fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need a JS adapter thing for the rich text editor we're bundling. Basically right now devs have to hand-write all the script tags, startup calls, plugin registrations etc. and it's a mess — people keep getting the asset URLs wrong, forgetting to strip version strings in certain places but not others, and the form rendering variables are all over the place.

The idea is: you describe what you want once, and the system spits out the exact JavaScript snippets or form-view data you need. Think of it like that asset-normalization pattern we used in the login module — same idea where some URLs need the query string removed and some don't, depending on context.

It needs to handle: resolving editor asset URLs, generating the CKEDITOR startup call with proper locale handling (remember we have those locale codes with underscores that need normalizing), registering plugins/styles/templates as separate snippets, managing named config collections with merge support, and producing form view variables for templates.

One thing that kept biting us before — raw JS expressions like regex literals inside config need to come out unquoted, not as JSON strings. And when the editor is disabled the form view should be trimmed down, not the full payload.

The whole thing should be testable via stdin/stdout JSON commands. Missing named resources should give a clean neutral error, not some internal exception message. Shouldn't be a single giant file — keep concerns separated properly.

Quick follow-up from the questions that came in: the URL cleanup rule is intentionally split depending on whether we’re talking about a directory-style path or an actual script file. Only the base_path has its query string stripped. The js_path keeps the full query string, including version/cache bits. So '/static/editor/?v=2' should become '/static/editor/' for base_path, but '/static/editor/editor.js?v=2' stays exactly as-is for js_path. Same rule should be reused in the asset URL normalization work and also when resolving plugin paths and contentsCss.

Also on locale handling, when we get locale strings with underscores like 'pt_BR', including when they come from locale_source='request_stack', they need to be converted to the format CKEditor expects by replacing underscores with hyphens and lowercasing the entire thing. So 'pt_BR' becomes 'pt-br', and that is the value that should land in the 'language' config key.

For named config output, the empty case should be exactly three lines: 'has_entries=no\ndefault=null\nentries=[]\n'. If there are entries, then has_entries=yes, default should show the configured default name or null, and entries should be a JSON object, not an array.

On the stdin/stdout JSON side, please keep that adapter physically and logically separate from the core logic. The adapter should just translate JSON commands into method calls on the core system. The core system should never import or depend on JSON parsing or stdin/stdout I/O directly. So this should land as a multi-file setup with distinct modules for core logic, I/O adaptation, and formatting, not one big mixed file.

One more important serialization detail: configuration values that are actually JavaScript expressions need to come out verbatim, not JSON-quoted. The specific cases here are regex literals in protectedSource arrays and selector/parser expressions. For example, '/<\?[\s\S]*?\?>/g' should be emitted as /<\?[\s\S]*?\?>/g with no surrounding quotes, while normal string values should still be JSON encoded.

And for contentsCss, those values need to go through the asset layer first with query strings stripped, then be emitted as a JSON array. The slash escaping matters here too: forward slashes in the resolved URL should appear as '\/' in the JSON output. So '/assets/theme.css' becomes '\/assets\/theme.css' inside the JSON string.

trigger_keywords: destroy script guard
teardown before deleting instance
how to safely remove editor instance
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The destroy script must check for instance existence before acting: 'if (CKEDITOR.instances["<id>"]) { CKEDITOR.instances["<id>"].destroy(true); delete CKEDITOR.instances["<id>"]; }'. The guard, destroy call with true, and delete must all be present.

trigger_keywords: plugin path query string
addExternal path stripping
plugin directory URL normalization
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Plugin paths registered via CKEDITOR.plugins.addExternal must have their query strings stripped (directory-style paths), matching the same rule as base_path normalization. So '/static/wordcount/?v=2' becomes '/static/wordcount/' in the emitted script.

trigger_keywords: missing config error format
neutral error message for missing name
no exception class names in output
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When a named resource is not found, the output must be exactly two lines: 'error=missing_config\nname=<requested_name>\n'. No Python/host-language exception class names, stack traces, or runtime messages should appear. The error category is always the neutral string 'missing_config'.

trigger_keywords: named config merge behavior
merge override keys
later keys win in merge
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When a merge operation is applied to a named config, only the keys specified in the merge value are overwritten. Keys present in the original but absent from the merge value are preserved. For example, merging {uiColor: '#000000'} into {toolbar: 'basic', uiColor: '#ffffff'} produces {toolbar: 'basic', uiColor: '#000000'}.

trigger_keywords: form view when disabled
disabled editor view payload
what fields appear when enable is false
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When the editor is disabled (enable=false), the form view output must contain ONLY {"enable":false} — all other editor-specific rendering variables (asset paths, config, plugins, styles, templates, filebrowsers, flags) are omitted entirely from the view JSON.

trigger_keywords: default form view values
initial view flags
what are the out-of-the-box view variables
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The default form_view output (no defaults or overrides applied) must include exactly these fields with these values: enable=true, async=false, autoload=true, auto_inline=true, inline=false, jquery=false, require_js=false, input_sync=false, filebrowsers=[], base_path='bundles/ivoryckeditor/', js_path='bundles/ivoryckeditor/ckeditor.js', jquery_path='bundles/ivoryckeditor/adapters/jquery.js', config=[], plugins=[], styles=[], templates=[].

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: do you have test cases

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

trigger_keywords: what are the valid action field values
what values can action take
list all supported action values
what actions does the dispatcher handle
which action strings does the adapter support
what are the possible action inputs
tell me all the action names
what operations are supported
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The dispatcher routes on the 'action' field. Valid values are: 'asset_paths', 'widget_script', 'destroy_script', 'plugin_script', 'named_configs', 'read_config', 'form_view'. Each value maps to one feature.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the form view should be trimmed down
context_pointers.actual_meaning: When defaults contain enable=false, the form_view response must output only {"enable":false} — every other view variable (paths, flags, resource arrays) is suppressed. This is the disabled-editor short-circuit path in Feature 7.

The read_config missing-entry error output format is strictly 'error=missing_config\nname=<name>\n' — no exception class names, no Python tracebacks, no KeyError or similar language-runtime messages. Feature 6 named resource collections.

oracle_data.fallback_response: That's not something I have specific details on from the original brief. I'd suggest looking at the existing test cases in the rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ directory and the behavior described for similar features — the pattern there should give you enough signal to extrapolate the correct behavior.

feature1_asset_url_normalization.json.description: Asset-backed editor resource paths are rendered as browser URLs, with cache query strings removed only where a directory-style base path is required.

feature2_widget_script_generation.json.description: Editor startup scripts render the target element id, normalized language data, selected startup mode, and input synchronization code as JavaScript.

feature3_content_css_and_filebrowser_url.description: Widget configuration converts stylesheet asset paths and browser route declarations into the final JavaScript configuration URLs.

feature4_raw_javascript_configuration_va.description: Configuration values that are intended to be JavaScript expressions are emitted without JSON string quoting while ordinary values remain JSON encoded.

feature5_auxiliary_registration_scripts..description: Auxiliary editor resources are rendered as JavaScript snippets that register plugins, style sets, template sets, and teardown behavior.

feature6_named_resource_collections.json.description: Named collections preserve supplied entries, replace entries with the same name, merge selected editor configuration data, and expose neutral missing-entry errors.

feature7_form_view_defaults_and_override.description: Creating an editor form view exposes default view variables, applies configured defaults, accepts per-form overrides, and omits editor-only variables when disabled.
